title: Exchanging
subtitle: Every trade changes the position
summary: Learn to ask what a trade removes, opens, or improves before capturing.
attribution: Original prose; ideas after Nimzowitsch, My System. No book text reproduced.

steps.title: A trade is a decision
steps.markdown: An [[exchange]] is not just arithmetic. Before you capture, ask what disappears from the board and what new squares become available.

steps.title: Take the profitable trade
steps.markdown: This trade is not subtle: a knight can remove the queen. Find the capture.

hints.text: The knight on b3 attacks d4.
A knight can jump to the queen.

feedback.correct: Right. This exchange wins major material, so the capture is justified.
feedback.wrong: Look for the knight capture on the queen.

steps.title: The exchange question
steps.markdown: What should you ask before making any less obvious exchange?

options.label: What changes after both sides capture?
options.explain: Yes. Count material, but also check activity, weak squares, open lines, and defenders.

options.label: Can I capture something immediately?
options.explain: That is only the start. The opponent's reply matters just as much.

options.label: Can I remove my active piece from the board?
options.explain: Trading your active piece for a passive one often helps the opponent.

steps.title: Trade with a reason
steps.markdown: A good exchange wins material, removes a defender, opens a line, or improves your remaining pieces. If a trade does none of those things, slow down.
